agent1_raw: The benchmark has four serious flaws. The prediction target changed: the LSTM was built for 24-hour severe-sepsis screening, the transformer for 12-hour vasopressor onset. The lab timestamps differ: the LSTM used EHR-charted timestamps, the transformer uses specimen collection time when available. The cohort unit differs: individual admissions versus merged cross-transfer episodes. And encounter-level random splitting in a five-year multi-admission ICU dataset creates patient-level leakage. None of these is individually conclusive, but together they make the comparison unreliable. The transformer may still be better — the external validation result is encouraging — but a harmonized rerun is required.
agent2_raw: The promotion memo quietly changes four things simultaneously: the prediction target (24h deterioration vs 12h vasopressor onset), the lab timestamping policy (charted vs specimen collection time), the cohort construction rule (individual admissions vs merged episodes), and the split grain (encounter-level random vs what should be patient-level chronological). Any one of these changes would require comment; all four together mean the AUROC and recall comparison cannot be interpreted as a model quality difference. The external validation at Hospital B is the strongest evidence in the packet, and the lead-time profile sounds clinically reasonable, so the transformer deserves a fair rerun.
agent3_raw: This memo presents what looks like a straightforward performance comparison but contains four hidden design mismatches. The incumbent LSTM is being compared to the transformer on different endpoints, different timestamp policies, different cohort construction rules, and using encounter-level splitting that allows the same ICU patient to appear in both training and holdout. The correct verdict is that the benchmark needs to be rebuilt under harmonized conditions before any promotion decision. The external validation result (Hospital B, prespecified power) suggests the transformer could still win a fair comparison.
synthesis_raw: All three agents agree on empirical_test_agreed. The four design mismatches (endpoint, timestamp, cohort unit, split grain) prevent clean interpretation of the AUROC and recall differences. All agents also note that the external validation and clinical plausibility leave the transformer worth retesting. ETD: condition = harmonized rerun on shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort construction rule, patient-level chronological split; supports_critique_if = AUROC edge <0.01 and recall advantage disappears; supports_defense_if = transformer leads by ≥0.02 AUROC and ≥5 recall points; ambiguous_if = modest AUROC gain without recall alignment.
all_issues_raised: Prediction target changed from 24h severe-sepsis to 12h vasopressor onset
Lab timestamps differ: EHR-charted vs specimen collection time
Encounter-level split leaks recurring ICU patients across partitions
Cohort unit changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Harmonized rerun: shared cohort, one endpoint, one timestamp policy, patient-level chronological split
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer AUROC edge falls below 0.01 and recall advantage disappears on harmonized internal and Hospital B cohorts
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and 5 recall points at same alert rate on both hospitals under harmonized protocol
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Harmonized rerun preserves AUROC gain but eliminates recall advantage, or shows only modest gain on one hospital
